claim: When agents have sufficient capability (GPT 5.2+), code becomes abundant and free to produce; the scarce resources shift to human time, human attention, and model context window
actionable: In agent-driven development, stop optimizing for code generation quality; instead optimize for human oversight, attention allocation, and context usage
source_title: Harness Engineering: How to Build Software When Humans Steer, Agents Execute

claim: Harnesses are systems that provide agents with right context at the right time; they should minimize upfront context loading and instead use just-in-time instruction injection via lint errors, test failures, and review agents
actionable: Design harnesses to defer non-critical instructions until later stages (lint, test, review phases) rather than front-loading all requirements, reducing initial context burden
source_title: Harness Engineering: How to Build Software When Humans Steer, Agents Execute

claim: Encoding good software engineering practices as custom lint rules, test assertions, and review agent instructions makes those practices automatically enforced across all agent outputs without human review effort
actionable: Convert best practices into custom linters and test assertions; agents will self-correct their code to pass these checks without human intervention
source_title: Harness Engineering: How to Build Software When Humans Steer, Agents Execute

claim: Review agents should run on every push, checking code against documented non-functional requirements, preventing humans from having to repeatedly give feedback on the same issues
actionable: Create review agents that enforce documented best practices (e.g., retries on network calls, type safety) automatically on every PR, catching issues before human review
source_title: Harness Engineering: How to Build Software When Humans Steer, Agents Execute

claim: Skills (reusable agent capabilities) should be kept minimal (5-10) and broad rather than numerous and specific, avoiding the need to constantly update skill definitions as tools and infrastructure change
actionable: Design agent skills to be broadly applicable; hide complexity beneath the skill abstraction to minimize breaking changes when infrastructure updates
source_title: Harness Engineering: How to Build Software When Humans Steer, Agents Execute

claim: Structuring code repositories with many small, domain-isolated packages (750 in one case) makes it easier for agents to scope their changes locally and reuse transferable patterns consistently
actionable: Organize monorepos into isolated packages by domain/layer; agents will find it easier to locate relevant code and apply consistent patterns
source_title: Harness Engineering: How to Build Software When Humans Steer, Agents Execute

claim: Making code uniform (one ORM, one language, one way to do async helpers, one CI pattern) improves agent reliability because it reduces the variety of tokens the model needs to predict
actionable: Enforce consistency across your codebase; agents will produce more predictable, higher-quality output with less context needed
source_title: Harness Engineering: How to Build Software When Steer, Agents Execute

claim: When agents produce high velocity (3-5 PRs per engineer per day), minimizing merge conflicts requires not just code isolation but also minimizing PR lifetime by automating code review
actionable: Implement automated review agents to reduce PR open time; combine with repository restructuring to minimize merge conflicts at scale
source_title: Harness Engineering: How to Build Software When Humans Steer, Agents Execute

claim: Every human interaction with an agent (e.g., clicking 'continue') indicates a failure of the harness to provide sufficient context; well-designed harnesses minimize these explicit interventions
actionable: Treat every 'continue' prompt as a harness design failure; iteratively improve context provision and guardrail clarity to reduce interruptions
source_title: Harness Engineering: How to Build Software When Humans Steer, Agents Execute

claim: QA plans documented by domain experts (product-minded engineers) can be enforced by review agents, multiplying the expertise across all agent trajectories without humans needing to manually teach each agent
actionable: Have domain experts document QA practices, test structures, and feature validation in the repository; review agents will enforce these patterns on all new code
source_title: Harness Engineering: How to Build Software When Humans Steer, Agents Execute

claim: Error messages from lint or test failures should provide remediation instructions, not just report the problem; agents will self-correct based on clear next steps
actionable: Write lint and test failure messages as instructions: 'use Zod for types at the edge' rather than just 'unknown type detected'
source_title: Harness Engineering: How to Build Software When Humans Steer, Agents Execute

claim: The future of agent-driven development requires moving security and QA testing earlier into the SDLC; white-box observability and tracing are necessary for agents to test long-running executions (not just chat inputs/outputs)
actionable: As agents handle more complex tasks, invest in internal observability (tracing, logging) so agents can validate behavior across the full execution path
source_title: Harness Engineering: How to Build Software When Humans Steer, Agents Execute

claim: Code can be framed as a compiled artifact; LLMs act like fuzzy compilers that apply constraints and optimization passes (lints, tests, review agents) to produce acceptable code, similar to LLVM optimization
actionable: Think of constraints, lint rules, and test requirements as compilation passes; they define acceptable code shapes independent of the underlying model
source_title: Harness Engineering: How to Build Software When Humans Steer, Agents Execute
